AHT vs Quality Relationship
⬤ Efficient
Low AHT + High Accuracy
Fast and accurate — benchmark these reviewers.
⬤ Careful
High AHT + High Accuracy
Accurate but slow — speed coaching only.
⬤ Rushing
Low AHT + Low Accuracy
Cutting corners — slow down, check policy.
⬤ Struggling
High AHT + Low Accuracy
Both issues — prioritise accuracy gap first.

Reviewer mix = share of decisions from reviewers landing in each achievement band for that SW (hover for breakdown): Exceeding Achieving Approaching Needs improvement
Team mix = one segment per TL, sized by their agent count on this SW. A diagonal hatch overlay marks the share of that TL's agents not meeting goal (denser hatch = higher share). Hover a segment for the TL's name and exact count. Kwon Wen Jie Apple Karl Others

Defect Intelligence

Identify policies not improving, reviewers driving repeat markdowns, and systemic vs isolated gaps.

How This Works
How strikes are counted
Every time a reviewer makes an error on the same policy, the strike counter increments. Strikes are per policy, per reviewer — the same policy error across different sub-workflows counts as one pattern. The counter is lifetime and never resets.
Strike status — based on recency
ActiveError occurred within 60 days — needs coaching action now
WatchError occurred 61–120 days ago — monitor for recurrence
HistoricalError occurred 120+ days ago — on record, resolved
How to read the Strike badge
Each badge shows counts per status — only non-zero segments are shown.
Active 1 Watch 5 Hist 3
= 1 recent error, 5 fading, 3 resolved — 9 lifetime total
Reviewer
Policy Risk — Not Improving
PolicyRepeatsTrendStatusType
What it shows
Policies with repeat errors ranked by urgency. Systemic = ≥2 reviewers failing the same policy.
What it means
Active policies need immediate coaching. Systemic policies indicate a calibration gap — not just one reviewer.
Next step
Start with Active + Systemic. These affect multiple reviewers and are unresolved. Add to team agenda.
Sub-workflow Risk — Not Improving
Sub-workflowRepeatsTrendStatusType
What it shows
Sub-workflows with concentrated repeat errors — where the queue itself may be driving mistakes.
What it means
High-active SWs suggest queue-level difficulty or insufficient calibration for that content type.
Next step
Cross-reference with Policy Risk. If the same SW dominates both tables, target that queue for a team calibration session.
Defect Trajectory — Top Active Policies (monthly error count)
What it shows
Monthly repeat error count for the top 5 active policies over the last 6 months.
What it means
Rising line = policy not improving. Flat = plateau. Declining = coaching may be working. Sudden spike = calibration event or new content type.
Next step
Rising lines are your highest-priority coaching topics. Declining lines still need monitoring until they reach zero.
Historical Defect Log All Time · All Reviewers
What it shows
Every individual error row across all reviewers, all time. Each row is one defect occurrence on a policy.
What it means
The Strike badge shows the full picture for that reviewer+policy: how many errors are Active, Watch, and Historical in total.
Next step
Filter to Active to see only current coaching targets. Use the SW pills to narrow to a specific queue.
ReviewerPolicySWs hit ErrorsStrike StatusLast Error

Improve & Control Phase

Comparing most recent two months · All TLs · All Tenures

Low n <30 decisions
Status key: SustainedPassing acc & AHT both periods ImprovedNow passing, wasn't before MonitoringBorderline or AHT-only issue RegressedAccuracy dropped below target

Acc delta is volume-weighted by current month decisions. Low n <30 decisions — treat with caution.

1Load Roster and Queue Map first — without them, quality and AHT data will not be read by the toolkit
2For quality: upload NRT Precision CSV (Tableau) or Quality Extract CSV — either populates the NRT QA tab
3For AHT: download the User Metrics data from SRT, then upload as SRT Metrics below
